Catalytic Growth of b-Ga2O3 Nanowires by Arc
Discharge**
By Young Chul Choi, Won Seok Kim, Young Soo Park,
Seung Mi Lee, Dong Jae Bae, Young Hee Lee,*
Gyeong-Su Park, Won Bong Choi, Nae Sung Lee, and
Jong Min Kim
Recently, nanotubes or nanowires have been successfully
grown using various materials and have received attention
due to their mesoscopic phases, which provide new physical
properties and strong applications for devices. However,
from the energetic point of view, it is rather unusual to
have nanostructures compared with bulk phases. Nonethe-
less, various nanotubes or nanowires are observed experi-
mentally, although the formation mechanism is far from
being clearly understood. Four independent growth mecha-
nisms have been suggested for the growth of nanowires and
nanotubes: i) vapor±liquid±solid (VLS) growth, where
nanowires continue to grow via eutectic alloys existing at
the tip of nanowires;[1±3] ii) catalytic growth assisted by a
small amount of transition metal;[4±6] iii) template-induced
growth, where carbon nanotubes are utilized as a tem-
plate;[7±9] iv) oxide-assisted growth, where oxides play a
dominant role in the nucleation and growth of high-quality
semiconductor nanowires.[10] The key issue here is how to
compensate for the energy penalty in forming nanowires
and further accelerate kinetically the formation of nano-
structures. We focus in particular here on the role of transi-
tion metals, which seem to be an essential ingredient in
forming nanostructures. Unfortunately, the transition met-
als play different roles with different synthesis approaches.
Therefore, a comprehensive understanding of the role of
transition metals for a given synthesis approach is required
in order to control the growth process.
Monoclinic gallium oxide (b-Ga2O3) is chemically and
thermally stable. It is useful for insulating oxide layers for
all gallium-based semiconductors. Furthermore, it can be
±
[*] Prof. Y. H. Lee, Y. C. Choi, W. S. Kim, Y. S. Park, S. M. Lee,
Dr. D. J. Bae
Department of Semiconductor Science and Technology
and Semiconductor Physics Research Center
Jeonbuk National University
Jeonju 561-756 (Republic of Korea)
Prof. Y. H. Lee
Department of Physics
Jeonbuk National University
Jeonju 561-756 (Republic of Korea)
Dr. G.-S. Park
Analytical Engineering Laboratory
Samsung Advanced Institute of Technology
PO Box 111, Suwon 440-600 (Republic of Korea)
Dr. W. B. Choi, Dr. N. S. Lee, Dr. J. M. Kim
Display Laboratory
Samsung Advanced Institute of Technology
PO Box 111, Suwon 440-600 (Republic of Korea)
[**] This project was supported in part by the Korea Research Foundation
through the Semiconductor Physics Research Center at Jeonbuk
National University, and in part by the BK21 program.
 applied as an optical limiter for ultravio-
let and also as an oxygen gas sensor. In
particular, a large surface area/volume
ratio is necessary for sensor applications.
Therefore, it is important to have a nano-
wire form of gallium oxide if possible.
We synthesized b-Ga2O3 nanowires by
direct current (DC) arc discharge of
GaN powders with a small amount of
transition metal in an argon/oxygen am-
bient
atmosphere.
With
high-resolu-
tion transmission electron microscopy
(HRTEM) measurements, we were able
to observe some pre-matured nanowires
that provided clear clues to the forma-
tion process. GaN powder was trans-
formed catalytically into gallium oxides
by the oxidation reaction assisted by the
transition metal particles. Without the
transition metals, neither gallium oxide
wire nor oxide particles were formed.
Ga2O3 nanowires start growing from the
twin defects at the center of the wire.
The prematured nanowires reveal rough
step edges, whereas the completed nano-
wires show a clean surface without steps.
This strongly suggests that step-growth
takes place in association with the transi-
tion metals, which enhance an anisotropic diffusion along
the direction of the wire axis, resulting in the formation of
nanowires.
b-Ga2O3 nanowires were synthesized with a conven-
tional DC arc-discharge method. GaN powder (5 N, High
Purity Chemicals) with 5 wt.-% of transition metal pow-
ders (Ni/Co = 1:1 and Ni/Co/Y = 4.5:4.5:1.0) was pressed
into a small hole (diameter: 4 mm, depth: 25 mm) of the
graphite anode (diameter: 6 mm).[11] Direct current of
55±65 A with a voltage of 13±15 V was applied between
two electrodes under a total pressure of 500 torr of argon
and oxygen gases (pAr/pO2 = 400:100). The arcing time was
typically 5±6 s. The chamber wall was kept cool by circulat-
ing water. Nanowires were deposited on the cathode,
chamber wall, and in-between in the form of a web-like
network. Bluish gray powders were obtained from three re-
gions, although the original GaN powder was white in col-
or. In addition, a small amount of carbon nanotubes was
detected sometimes as a black powder. Arcing for too long
a time produces unnecessary carbon nanotubes. Bluish gray
powders were extracted exclusively for analyses.
Figure 1a shows a typical scanning electron microscopy
(SEM) (JMS 6400, JEOL) image of a web-like network. A
large quantity of nanowires are formed having relatively
uniform diameters. The nanowires are straight and long, re-
sulting in a very large aspect ratio. We observed from cath-
ode deposits that nanowires are as long as 100 mm in some
cases.[12] We observed a very high yield of nanowires in our
case, in good contrast with carbon nanotubes generated by
arc-discharge, where a large number of carbonaceous parti-
cles were produced in addition to carbon nanotubes.[13,14] It
was found that the addition of a small amount of yttrium
was necessary for a massive production of nanowires, sug-
gesting that yttrium may play a part as an effective catalyst.
The chamber pressure is also a decisive factor. Nanowires
cannot be formed at a pressure below 450 torr. We empha-
size that no nanowire was formed without transition met-
als.
Figure 1b shows a typical HRTEM (H-9000NA, Hitachi)
image. The image clearly illustrates the crystalline structure
of a nanowire with twin defects (twin angle of about 160)
at the center of the nanowire. The nanowire reveals a
smooth surface without step edges. The typical diameter of
the nanowires is about 33 nm. No amorphous layer was
formed on the surface, in good contrast with the previous
reports for Si, SiC, and GaN nanowires.[2,3,9] The inset
shows
the
Fourier
diffractogram
obtained
from
the
HRTEM image of Figure 1b, indicating a crystalline nature
and the direction of the nanowire axis to be [001]. In order
to confirm this, we constructed a b-Ga2O3 nanowire and
obtained a simulated pattern for the HRTEM image, as
shown in Figure 1c, again clearly demonstrating similar
properties. The crystallographic directions in the simulated
pattern agree with the observed HRTEM measurements
with lattice parameters of a = 12.23 , b = 3.04 , c =
5.8  (a = 90, b = 103.7, g = 90). The distance between

HRTEM image of the completely formed nanowire and (inset) the Fourier diffractogram obtained
from the HRTEM image; c) simulated image of b-Ga2O3 nanowires with 6 ´ 6 ´ 12 supercell;
d) HRTEM image of pre-matured nanowire showing step edges.

into a small hole (diameter: 4 mm, depth: 25 mm) of the
graphite anode (diameter: 6 mm).[11] Direct current of

and oxygen gases (pAr/pO2 = 400:100). The arcing time was

gions, although the original GaN powder was white in col-
or. In addition, a small amount of carbon nanotubes was
detected sometimes as a black powder. Arcing for too long

large quantity of nanowires are formed having relatively
uniform diameters. The nanowires are straight and long, re-
sulting in a very large aspect ratio. We observed from cath-
ode deposits that nanowires are as long as 100 mm in some
cases.[12] We observed a very high yield of nanowires in our

Figure 1b shows a typical HRTEM (H-9000NA, Hitachi)
image. The image clearly illustrates the crystalline structure
of a nanowire with twin defects (twin angle of about 160)
at the center of the nanowire. The nanowire reveals a
smooth surface without step edges. The typical diameter of
the nanowires is about 33 nm. No amorphous layer was
formed on the surface, in good contrast with the previous
reports for Si, SiC, and GaN nanowires.[2,3,9] The inset
HRTEM image of Figure 1b, indicating a crystalline nature
and the direction of the nanowire axis to be [001]. In order
to confirm this, we constructed a b-Ga2O3 nanowire and
obtained a simulated pattern for the HRTEM image, as
shown in Figure 1c, again clearly demonstrating similar
properties. The crystallographic directions in the simulated
pattern agree with the observed HRTEM measurements
with lattice parameters of a = 12.23 , b = 3.04 , c =

bright lines along the wire axis is 6.1  in the simulated
pattern, in good agreement with about 6  in the HRTEM
image (Fig. 1b). Figure 1d shows an interesting nanowire,
which again includes twin defects at the center, with rough
step edges on the surface and a diameter of 15 nm. This
suggests that this nanowire is pre-matured with rough step
edges that will eventually end up as a smooth surface with
larger diameter, as shown in Figure 1b. The pre-matured
nanowires have diameters of 10 to 20 nm, whereas the
completed nanowires have diameters of 30 to 45 nm. We
note that some transition metals are present on the stepped
surface of nanowires with twin defects, which was con-
firmed by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDXRS)
measurement.[15] We also found that no nanowires were
formed without transition metals, indicating that the transi-
tion metals assisted step growth. Transition metals can ad-
here easily at the step edge.[16] The presence of transition
metals at the step edge may play an important role in con-
trolling the anisotropic diffusion, resulting in the formation
of nanowires with a smooth surface. The related growth
mechanism will be discussed in detail later.
Figure 2a shows an X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern
(PSPC-MDG2000, Rigaku) of the nanowires. The peak po-
sitions are in good agreement with those for b-Ga2O3 pow-
der obtained from the Joint Committee on Powder Diffrac-
tion Standards (JCPDS) card.[17] However, the relative
intensity is different, indicating the formation of quantum
nanowires. Strong peaks at the (312) and (5Å12) planes are
shown in nanowires, whereas the peak at the (111) plane
shows the highest intensity in b-Ga2O3 powder. Figure 2b
b-Ga2O3 powder (5 N, Aldrich). Several sharp peaks at
253, 321, 345, 476, 630, 653, and 767 cm±1 were observed in
addition to the previously reported peaks at 201 and
417 cm±1.[18] The peak positions for nanowires are in excel-
lent agreement with those for powders, although the peaks
become narrower, indicating good crystallinity. We note
that neither GaN nor carbon-related peaks are observed in
either the XRD pattern or the Raman spectrum. The
EDXRS was carried out with high spatial resolution of the
field-emission (FE) TEM and shows peaks of Ga and O to-
gether with the peaks of transition metals, as shown in Fig-
ure 2c, again indicating the formation of gallium oxide.[19]
C and Cu peaks are due to the carbon-coated copper grid
used for TEM measurements. It can be concluded from
these structural analyses that the observed nanowires are
monoclinic gallium oxide (b-Ga2O3) with [001] direction
Although b-Ga2O3 nanowires were clearly confirmed
from the above analyses, it is still not clear how b-Ga2O3
nanowires are formed from GaN powders either energeti-
cally or kinetically. Here, we evaluate Gibbs free energy of
the reaction. The reaction can be expressed as:
2GaN + (3/2 + x)O2 (g) ±? Ga2O3 + 2NOx (g)
In order for the above reaction to occur, the standard
Gibbs free energy (DGo) of the reaction should be negative.
To calculate the DGo of the reaction, we chose NO gas, i.e.,
x = 1, as a produced gas, and a reaction temperature of
5000 K.[20,21] DGo was then estimated using the hand-
book.[22] The calculated value, DGo = ±620 KJ/mol, indi-
cates that this reaction is energetically favorable.[23] Since
nanowires were not formed without transition metals, we
believe that transition metals play a crucial role in forming
nanowires. Furthermore, even Ga2O3 powders were not
formed without transition metals, suggesting that transition
metals catalytically promote the above reaction in trans-
forming GaN powders into gallium oxides. One may be-
lieve that nickel oxide and cobalt oxide can also be formed
during the reaction. However, standard Gibbs free energies
of the reactions for forming these oxides are positive at arc-
Fig. 2. a) XRD pattern of b-Ga2O3 nanowires produced by arc discharge,
and relative XRD intensities of b-Ga2O3 powders referenced from the
JCPDS card [17]. b) FT-Raman spectra of b-Ga2O3 nanowires produced by
arc discharge and b-Ga2O3 powder (5 N) purchased from Aldrich Co.
c) EDXRS spectrum of the obtained nanowires
ing temperature,[22] excluding the possibility of producing
The pre-matured thin nanowires were formed initially
with twin defects at the center of the wire, as shown in the
HRTEM images. From the pre-matured rough step edges
and the presence of transition metals on the stepped sur-
face, one may conjecture that the nanowire involves step
growth assisted by the transition metals. We also observed
that the addition of a small amount of yttrium resulted in a
larger quantity of nanowires, i.e., yttrium is more efficient
than Ni or Co in the formation of nanowires. This confirms
the step growth, where the step barrier height may be con-
trolled by the species of the transition metals present at the
step edge.[16] According to the VLS growth mechanism,[1±3]
the growth of nanowires is governed by the presence of eu-
tectic metal alloys at the end of nanowires. Transition met-
als adsorbed at the edge of carbon nanotubes play an im-
portant role during the growth of single-walled carbon
nanotubes.[4,5] However, in our case, the transition metals
are not present at the end of nanowires. Instead, we ob-
served the transition metals at the surface of the nanowires.
Based on these experimental observations, we now propose
a growth mechanism catalytically assisted by the transition
metals. GaN powders may be decomposed catalytically into
small clusters with the assistance of oxygen plasma before
gallium oxides are formed, as shown in Figure 3a. The gal-
lium oxides start to aggregate, forming nucleation seeds,
i.e., twin defects, at the center of the wire, as shown in Fig-
ures 1b and 1d. Step growth is involved in completing the
formation of the nanowires, as suggested in Figure 1d. The
step edge provides an extra surface diffusion barrier, called
the Schwoebel barrier, Esch,[24] as shown in Figure 3b. Tran-
sition metals favor adsorption at the step edge. This re-
duces Esch significantly,[16] enhancing an anisotropic diffu-
sion along the nanowire axis. Step growth continues to
occur, with the Ni atom being pushed outward by the de-
scending gallium oxide. Once the step growth is termi-
nated, clean surfaces are formed. Substitution of Ni by Y
will reduce the Schwoebel barrier more significantly due to
the larger atomic size and lower surface free energy, giving
rise to greater efficiency in producing nanowires. This phe-
nomenon is similar to the role of a surfactant (As, Bi, and
Sb) in Si/Ge epitaxial growth.[16] Lowering the diffusion
barrier through the presence of transition metals at the step
edge is the key factor in our model. However, it is not clear
when the growth of nanowires can be terminated. Further
investigation is required in order to understand fully the
In summary, we have grown monoclinic gallium oxide
(b-Ga2O3) nanowires by DC arc discharge of GaN powders
with small amounts of transition metals in a gaseous mix-
ture of argon and oxygen. We propose a catalyst-assisted
step growth mechanism that involves: i) the catalytic de-
composition of GaN powders and the transformation of
GaN into Ga2O3, and ii) enhanced step growth by the pres-
ence of transition metals at the step edge.

Single-Walled Carbon Nanotube±Polymer
Composites: Strength and Weakness**
By Pulickel M. Ajayan,* Linda S. Schadler,
Cindy Giannaris, and Angel Rubio
Carbon nanotubes are near ideal whiskers consisting of
folded graphene layers with cylindrical hexagonal lattice
structure. During growth, depending on the conditions in
which they are formed, they assemble either as multiwalled
co-axial tubules (multiwalled nanotubes, MWNTs) or as
bundles (ropes) consisting of individual cylinders (single-
walled nanotubes, SWNTs) packed in two-dimensional tri-
angular lattices. Recent experimental studies[1] and theoret-
ical modeling[2] have amply demonstrated that individual
(~1.2 TPa), stiffness and flexibility. Their application as
structural reinforcement, however, (specifically in polymer
composites) is going to depend on the ability to transfer
load from the matrix to the nanotubes.[3]
The techniques used to understand the load transfer and
the failure of nanotubes have been micro-Raman spectros-
copy[4] and a Kelly±Tyson approach to measuring the inter-
facial shear stress.[5] These studies have given some evi-
dence for good load transfer between the polymer and the
nanotubes. The Kelly±Tyson approach requires fragment-
ing the nanotubes, but it is unclear if the failures observed
in the MWNT and SWNT composites are failures of indi-
vidual tubes or local instabilities of the aggregates (MWNT
or SWNT bundles). Clearly for SWNTs, the failure could
result either from nanotubes pulling out of bundles or from
the actual fracture of individual nanotubes in the bundles.
Micro-Raman spectroscopy can also be used to under-
stand load transfer in MWNTs because the second-order
A1g (disorder-induced) Raman peak position shifts with ap-
plied strain. If the nanotubes are carrying strain, then the
Raman peak will have a large shift with applied composite
strain. Lourie and co-workers observed a 6 cm±1 shift in
MWNTs under hydrostatic compression using thermal
loading,[4] and even larger shifts for SWNT samples.[6]
Schadler et al.[7] observed a 6 cm±1 shift in MWNTs in com-
pression and no shift in tension, implying that load transfer
in tension to the MWNTs was negligible. This was attribut-
ed to the fact that the inner tubes were sliding within the
outer tubes and hence the load was not effectively trans-
mitted to all the layers in the coaxial MWNT assembly, or
due to possible extremely low interfacial shear stresses be-
tween the tubes and the matrix arising from poor interfa-
cial bonding. This mechanism of deformation has been con-
firmed by pulling individual MWNTs to failure using two
atomic force microscopy (AFM) tips.[8]
In SWNTs the second-order A1g mode has been moni-
tored as a function of applied composite strain. Lourie and
co-workers observed a 21 wavenumber shift per percen-
tage strain in SWNT composites using thermal loading.[4]
More recent work by Wood et al. has shown significant Ra-
man shifts for SWNTs in hydrostatic compression using a
diamond anvil cell.[6] It is unclear, however, if this shift in-
dicates radial strain on individual SWNTs; it is more likely
that a shift in this mode indicates radial strain in the nano-
Our experiments here deal with observations of local
elastic behavior of individual single-walled nanotube bun-
dles and load transfer in epoxy composites as well as
pressed pellets of composites containing SWNTs and car-
bonaceous soot material formed during nanotube synthesis.
